natural_sources: Naturally derived from benzoic acid found in cranberries
natural_occurrence.description: The potassium salt of benzoic acid. While benzoic acid occurs naturally in various fruits and spices, potassium benzoate is produced synthetically for commercial use. Most effective as a preservative in acidic conditions (pH below 4.5).

manufacturing.description: Produced by neutralizing benzoic acid with potassium hydroxide or potassium carbonate. Benzoic acid is synthesized from toluene through partial oxidation.

other_uses.cosmetics: Used as a preservative in cosmetics and personal care products

eu.conditions: Maximum levels typically 150-6000 mg/kg depending on food type; concern about benzene formation when combined with ascorbic acid

usa.notes: Listed as indirect food additive for polymers in food contact surfaces; FDA has not defined intake recommendations for direct use

japan.notes: Part of benzoate group with usage standards

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–5 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates)
safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: 5 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates)

regulatory_history.event: FDA issues guidance on benzene formation in beverages containing benzoates and ascorbic acid
JECFA and EFSA confirm group ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day for benzoic acid and benzoates

equivalents.product: cans of soft drink (355ml at typical levels)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA group ADI for benzoic acid and benzoates. Key concern is benzene formation when combined with ascorbic acid (vitamin C) in the presence of heat or light, though levels in regulated beverages are typically well below safety thresholds (< 5 ppb).
